step_by_step_thinking: I largely concur with the assessment but want to emphasize why we can confidently lean towards **Diabetes Insipidus**, especially since the patient has been exclusively breastfed. Breast milk generally contains moderate sodium content; thus, significant hypernatremia unlikely results solely from diet unless there’s underlying pathology causing altered fluid balance. Furthermore, the presence of high urinary sodium supports the notion of inadequate antidiuretic hormone action seen in central diabetes insipidus, facilitating excessive water loss despite adequate hydration input through breast milk. Therefore, no need to consider severe dehydration as primary etiology based on presented data alone. Disagree slightly because although the case fits diabetes insipidus quite well, another consideration should also be made regarding possible congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH), particularly if cortisol deficiency leads to salt-wasting crisis alongside electrolyte imbalances. This could explain similar presentations yet requires different management strategies compared to DI. Thus, while diabetes insipidus appears highly likely, differential diagnoses must include CAH until ruled out definitively through biochemical testing. My final answer choice will remain **A**: Diabetes Insipidus, acknowledging broader possibilities within neonatal endocrinology contexts. Agree partially but add a critical nuance about differential diagnosis. Final
